my_thoughts: 'I think I'm very involved'—proforma claim contradicted by everything before. But now teacher uses their profession (lawyer) against them: 'If an intern sat like this in YOUR office, how involved?' Brilliant move: can't defend themselves without contradicting their own professional judgment. They judge interns by engagement; now judge SELF by same standard. The 'she' is clever—hypothetical third person, less threatening than 'you.' But the parallel is obvious: slouching, avoiding eyes, sarcastic. They'll have to admit low involvement or contradict their professional standards. Prediction: they'll admit it ('Not too involved, I guess').
accumulated_insight: T5: Student claims high involvement: 'I think I'm very involved'—proforma defense replaces sarcasm. But body language (slouching, sarcasm T4) contradicts verbal claim. Teacher uses professional analogy: 'If a prospective intern were sitting like this...how involved would you say she was?' This is RM_01: structural analogy using student's own professional standards (lawyer). Shifts from teacher-judging-student to student-judging-hypothetical-intern. Predicting ask_deeper_question because structural analogies typically produce recognition or questions.
visible_response:
You said you’re a lawyer.
If a prospective intern were sitting like this in your office for an interview, how involved would you say she was?
